retrieved_docs.title: Aducanumab -- Indications -- FDA Conditional Approval Initially Granted in July 2021 under the Program for Accelerated Approval
retrieved_docs.content: Biogen first presented positive results from its phase 1b study of aducanumab in early 2015; they enrolled the first subject in a global phase 3 clinical trial approximately 6 months later. In autumn 2019, Biogen announced plans for a regulatory filing; the Biologics License Application (BLA) was submitted to the FDA in July 2020 after delays caused by the early stages of the coronavirus pandemic.

retrieved_docs.title: Imdevimab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: As of January 24, 2022, according to the United States Food and Drug Association (FDA) fact sheet for imdevimab, "due to the high frequency of the Omicron variant, imdevimab is not currently authorized for use in any U.S. region because of markedly reduced activity against the omicron variant. This drug may not be administered for treatment or post-exposure prevention of COVID-19 under the Emergency Use Authorization until further notice by the Agency." This topic is to be used only for a historical review of the development and use of this drug.

retrieved_docs.title: Etesevimab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: As of January 24, 2022, the United States Food and Drug Associated (FDA) fact sheet for the drug etesevimab states that "due to the high frequency of the Omicron variant, bamlanivimab and etesevimab, administered together, are not currently authorized for use in any U.S. region because of markedly reduced activity against the Omicron variant. These drugs may not be administered for treatment or post-exposure prevention of COVID-19 under the Emergency Use Authorization until further notice by the Agency." This topic is for historical purposes only to review this therapy's indications, mechanism, and administration when it was first developed.

retrieved_docs.title: Etesevimab -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: There are no strict contraindications to using etesevimab. The following is a list of precautions or warnings while using Etesevimab and Bamlanivimab.

retrieved_docs.title: Aducanumab -- Indications -- FDA Conditional Approval Initially Granted in July 2021 under the Program for Accelerated Approval
retrieved_docs.content: In November 2020, the FDA's Peripheral and Central Nervous System Drugs Advisory Committee met to discuss the aducanumab BLA. A series of 4 votes culminated in the near-unanimous (there was a single abstention) verdict that the advisors did not find reasonable grounds to consider the results of the clinical trials as evidence of aducanumab's effectiveness for treating AD. Within days of the July 2021 accelerated approval decision, 3 members of the Advisory Committee, comprising one-third of its nongovernment members, resigned, citing "serious potential to impair future research" and deeming the FDA's ruling "the worst approval decision that FDA has made....." [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Bamlanivimab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Due to emerging variants, the FDA has authorized the combination drug to be used only in the states and territories where the data shows that resistance to the variants is < 5%. Healthcare providers should check the FDA website regularly for updates regarding this medication.

These drugs may not be administered for treatment or post-exposure prevention of COVID-19 under the Emergency Use Authorization until further notice by the Agency." This topic is for historical purposes only to review this therapy's indications, mechanism, and administration when it was first developed.

retrieved_docs.title: Etesevimab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The FDA (Food and Drug Administration) has approved both combination products through EUA (Emergency Use Authorizations), which can be used for mild to moderate SARS-CoV-2 in non-hospitalized patients at high risk for progressing to severe disease. FDA has revoked the use of bamlanivimab alone for SARS-COV-2 because of many resistant SARS-CoV-2 strains. The combination product (etesevimab and bamlanivimab) can be used in adults and pediatrics if they are at least 12 years of age and weigh 40 kg or more with mild to moderate SARS CoV-2. The patients are considered at high risk of progressing to severe COVID-19 or hospitalization if they satisfy 1 of the criteria below:

retrieved_docs.title: Luspatercept -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Luspatercept is an erythroid maturation agent approved by the FDA for treating anemia in patients with β-thalassemia who need regular red blood cell transfusions. [1] The recommended dose of luspatercept for patients with β-thalassemia is 1 mg/kg subcutaneously once every 3 weeks. [2] [3] The FDA approved this dosage based on the results of the phase III BELIEVE trial. [4] This medication promotes late-stage erythropoiesis, even in patients with β-thalassemia ineffectively controlled with erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESA).

retrieved_docs.title: Bamlanivimab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Bamlanivimab is a monoclonal antibody (mAb) directed against the spike protein (S-Protein) of severe acute respiratory syndrome-coronavirus-2 (SARS-CoV-2). Bamlanivimab, or LY-COV555, was derived from the convalescent plasma of patients with coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19). [1] It is 1 of the potent neutralizing antibodies approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) under Emergency Use Authorization (EUA) in August 2021 to be used in combination with etesivimab (another mAb against S-protein of SARS-CoV-2). [2] The other neutralizing antibodies available are casirivimab and imdevimab, used in combination therapy. [3] [4] [5] The other novel agent in this class is sotrovimab. [6] The FDA has approved the administration of the combination drug as treatment and post-exposure prophylaxis in patients meeting the following criteria:

retrieved_docs.title: Casirivimab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: As of January 24, 2022, according to the United States Food and Drug Association (FDA) fact sheet for casirivimab, "due to the high frequency of the Omicron variant, casirivimab is not currently authorized for use in any U.S. region because of markedly reduced activity against the omicron variant. This drug may not be administered for treatment or post-exposure prevention of COVID-19 under the Emergency Use Authorization until further notice by the Agency." This topic is to be used only for a historical review of the development and use of this drug.

retrieved_docs.title: Emicizumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Emicizumab was created to circumvent challenges associated with frequent intravenous administration of FVIII and offer a standardized treatment option for Hemophilia A patients with and without inhibitors. [4] Initially, Emicizumab was first approved by the FDA in 2017 for patients with congenital hemophilia A (in combination with inhibitors). Emcizicumab was subsequently approved for prophylaxis to prevent or reduce the frequency of bleeding episodes in patients with hemophilia A (congenital factor VIII deficiency) with or without factor VIII inhibitors. [5] Emicizumab was also granted priority review and breakthrough therapy designation by FDA. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Etesevimab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The combination product (bamlanivimab and etesevimab) must be given immediately after a positive test result and within 10 days of symptom onset. The combination product (etesevimab and bamlanivimab) is not authorized for hospitalized patients with SARS-CoV-2 or those patients requiring oxygen therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Best Practices For Administering Monoclonal Antibody Therapy For Coronavirus (COVID-19) (Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the race to decrease the global burden of COVID-19, several monoclonal antibodies were developed and granted emergency use authorizations (EUAs). However, as COVID-19 variants emerged, most of the monoclonal antibodies had their EUAs revoked due to limited efficacy against dominant circulating variants and subvariants. This activity reviews the pathophysiology and function of these monoclonal antibodies and the risks and benefits of these agents. However, it must be noted that according to the National Institutes of Health (NIH), none of these monoclonal antibodies are recommended in 2023 for the treatment of COVID-19. The only monoclonal antibody with an active EUA is tixagevimab co-packaged with cilgavimab, which is authorized for preexposure prophylaxis of COVID-19. [9] According to United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) update on 1/6/2023, a SARS-CoV-2 Omicron subvariant is not anticipated to be neutralized by this monoclonal antibody combination; however, they are waiting for additional data to make their final decision.

retrieved_docs.title: Federal Medication Development Regulation -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In 1961, thalidomide was shown to cause severe congenital disabilities after being marketed in Europe, prompting increased regulations on medication development. [4] There were also cases of thalidomide-induced congenital disabilities in the US. However, the FDA did not approve the medicine. The passage of the Kefauver-Harris Amendment in 1962 would allow the FDA to require proof that drugs were both safe and effective before approval. This amendment contained provisions for obtaining informed consent for clinical trials and initiated Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2782
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category encompasses systemic pharmacologic agents used to treat conditions characterized by airway obstruction, such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). By belonging to the same ATC class, these three drugs share a therapeutic focus on modulating inflammatory pathways or other mechanisms to improve airway function, highlighting their related roles in the management of obstructive airway disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 416
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system as drugs for obstructive airway diseases. Within this broad category, the ATC hierarchy defines two specialized subgroups: “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” and “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants.” The subgroup “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” is an inverse‑isa of the parent class “DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES,” indicating that it is a more specific category under the overall group. The same inverse‑isa relationship exists for the inhalant subgroup, and the inhalant subgroup is also explicitly linked to the parent class by an isa relationship, confirming its status as a direct child of the broader category. Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are members of the systemic subgroup, reflecting that they are administered systemically (e.g., oral, subcutaneous or intravenous routes) for the treatment of conditions such as asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and other obstructive airway disorders. Fenspiride is listed as a member of both the systemic subgroup and the inhalant subgroup, suggesting that it may be used either systemically or as an inhaled preparation in the management of obstructive airway disease. The inclusion of biologic agents such as benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab highlights the role of targeted monoclonal‑antibody therapies in severe asthma and related airway inflammation, while amlexanox’s placement in the systemic subgroup points to its potential off‑label or investigational use for airway disease. This ATC classification framework clarifies the therapeutic positioning of each drug, aids clinicians in selecting appropriate treatment routes, and supports consistent pharmacovigilance and prescribing practices for patients with obstructive airway diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 513
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are grouped in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) category “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases”, which also contains the subclass “Xanthines, systemic for obstructive airway disease”. Within the broader therapeutic concept “látky ovlivňující dýchací systém” (substances affecting the respiratory system), almitrin, aminofylin, benralizumab, bronchodilatancia, methapyrilen, theofylin, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are classified, indicating that all of these agents belong to the respiratory‑system‑affecting substance group. Bronchodilatancia is defined as a specific type of bronchodilation that inherits from the muscarinic agonist methacholin, whereas adrenergic β‑antagonists are explicitly contraindicated with methacholin, reflecting therapeutic incompatibility between β‑blockade and muscarinic stimulation. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2781
retrieved_docs.content: Methapyrilen, mepolizumab, and reslizumab are all classified in the therapeutic class of agents that affect the respiratory system. This class is a subset of the broader ATC category “other respiratory system products,” indicating that each of these drugs is considered a respiratory‑system agent within the ATC hierarchy. Recognizing that methapyrilen, mepolizumab, and reslizumab share this therapeutic classification helps clinicians understand their primary pharmacologic target—modulation of respiratory function—and supports appropriate selection and use in the management of respiratory disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 63
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system as members of the group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” Within the same ATC hierarchy, “DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES” is linked by inverse‑is‑a relationships to both “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” and “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants,” indicating that the systemic and inhalant categories are parallel sub‑classes of the broader obstructive‑airway‑disease drug class. Fenspiride is additionally listed as a member of the inhalant sub‑class, showing that it is used in both systemic and inhaled formulations for treating obstructive airway conditions. The ATC classification also reflects that the systemic‑drug group contains several monoclonal‑antibody therapies (benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, tezepelumab), underscoring the expanding role of targeted biologics

Overall, the knowledge base depicts a coherent structure in which a wide range of drugs—biologics such as benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab, as well as small molecules like roflumilast, methapyrilen, ardeparin, kromoglykát dvojsodný and bromodiphenhydramine—are unified under the therapeutic class of substances affecting the respiratory system and are classified together in the ATC system as systemic treatments for obstructive airway diseases. This integrated classification supports clinicians in recognizing the shared therapeutic purpose of these diverse agents and facilitates systematic prescribing, pharmacovigilance, and research within respiratory medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 697
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” category in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system. This grouping indicates that each of these agents is used systemically to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The list includes several monoclonal antibodies—benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab—that target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, as well as roflumilast, a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that helps control inflammation and bronchoconstriction. Amlexanox, although less commonly used for airway disease, is also placed in this category, reflecting its systemic therapeutic role. Grouping these diverse agents together highlights the range of systemic pharmacologic strategies available for managing obstructive airway diseases and underscores their shared classification despite differing mechanisms of action.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2718
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab and reslizumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This ATC category comprises systemic agents that are used to manage obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. By being members of the same pharmacological class, these drugs share a therapeutic purpose of improving airway function, although they achieve this through different mechanisms—benralizumab and mepolizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target the interleukin‑5 pathway, reslizumab also acts on the IL‑5 axis, and amlexanox exerts anti‑inflammatory effects. Recognizing that all four agents belong to this specific ATC group helps clinicians understand their role in the broader treatment strategy for obstructive airway diseases and facilitates appropriate drug selection and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1558
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC category “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This grouping indicates that each of these agents is a systemic medication used in the treatment of obstructive airway conditions, such as severe asthma, and they share a common therapeutic classification within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 400
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepirumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category groups systemic therapies that are used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The biologic agents benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepirumab target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, while roflumilast is a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that modulates inflammatory cell activity. Recognizing that these diverse agents belong to the same ATC class helps clinicians understand their shared therapeutic purpose and facilitates systematic prescribing, pharmacovigilance, and comparative effectiveness assessments in the management of obstructive airway diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 215
retrieved_docs.content: preparation label. The therapeutic area “Drugs used in hereditary angioedema” comprises ecallantide and icatibant, both biologic agents targeting bradykinin‑mediated angioedema. In the ATC system, aceneuramic acid, eteplirsen and nusinersen are grouped under “other drugs for disorders of the musculo‑skeletal system,” reflecting their application in musculo‑skeletal diseases. Nusinersen is the child of the “N [Preparations]” group and exerts a physiologic effect of increased protein synthesis, which underlies its therapeutic action. Collectively, these relationships delineate a structured taxonomy that links individual drugs to ATC categories, preparation hierarchies, pharmacologic classes (including inverse‑ISA relationships), and specific therapeutic indications, thereby providing a comprehensive view of their roles in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1595
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” Within this broader ATC category, these agents are also linked to the therapeutic class described in Czech as “látky ovlivňující dýchací systém” (substances affecting the respiratory system), which itself is a subset of the larger ATC group “other respiratory system products.” The therapeutic class “látky ovlivňující dýchací systém” includes benralizumab, methapyrilen, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab, indicating that these drugs share a common mechanism of action or therapeutic purpose related to modulation of respiratory pathways. Consequently, the classification system reflects that these medications—particularly the monoclonal antibodies benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab, which target interleukin‑5 or its receptor and are used for severe eosinophilic asthma—are grouped together both as systemic treatments for obstructive airway disease and as agents that specifically affect the respiratory system, while amlexanox, although mechanistically different, is also placed in the same ATC subgroup for its role in managing obstructive airway conditions. This integrated categorization aids clinicians in recognizing the shared therapeutic context of these drugs and facilitates appropriate selection and management of treatments for patients with obstructive airway diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2187
retrieved_docs.content: Inclisiran, evolocumab, and lomitapide are all classified as members of the “Other lipid modifying agents in ATC” category, indicating that each of these drugs belongs to the group of other lipid‑modifying agents within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical classification system. This shared classification reflects their common therapeutic purpose of modifying lipid levels, aiding clinicians in recognizing their related pharmacologic role.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 108
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC framework organizes therapies for obstructive airway diseases into a top‑level class “DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES,” which is subdivided into inhaled adrenergic agents and systemic agents. Inhaled adrenergic agents (“adrenergics, inhalants”) include the subclasses “alpha‑ and beta‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalants,” “non‑selective beta‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalants,” and “adrenergics in combination with anticholinergics incl. triple combinations with corticosteroids.” Fixed‑dose combination products in this subclass are Tiotropium Bromide/Olodaterol and Albuterol‑Ipratropium Bromide (the latter providing ipratropium). Systemic agents for obstructive airway disease comprise other systemic drugs such as amlexanox, the monoclonal antibodies benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, tezepelumab, and the leukotriene receptor antagonist seratrodast; fenspiride appears in both inhaled and systemic “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases” categories. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 382
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases”. These agents are used systemically to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma, and they represent a distinct therapeutic class within the broader ATC framework. Fenspiride is listed both as a member of this systemic group and as a member of the inhalant group “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants in ATC”, indicating that it can be administered by inhalation as well as having systemic activity. Within the inhalant category, the subclass “Antiallergic inhalants for obstructive airway diseases, excl. corticosteroids” is defined as a type of “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants in ATC”, highlighting non‑corticosteroid antiallergic inhaled therapies. This classification clarifies the relationships among systemic biologics, inhaled agents, and specific antiallergic inhalants, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate treatments based on route of administration and pharmacologic class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 60
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases”, indicating that they are systemic therapies used in conditions such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Metaproterenol is listed both as a non‑selective beta‑adrenoreceptor agonist for systemic use and as a non‑selective beta‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalant, reflecting its availability for systemic administration as well as inhalation. The hierarchy places non‑selective beta‑adrenoreceptor agonists for systemic use under the broader class “adrenergics for systemic use”, while non‑selective beta‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalants belong to the class “adrenergics, inhalants”. Alpha‑ and beta‑adrenoreceptor agonist inhalants are also a subclass of “adrenergics, inhalants”. The overarching category “DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES” is linked inversely to both “adrenergics, inhalants” and “adrenergics for systemic use”, and it is a subclass of “OTHER DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES, INHALANTS in ATC”. This classification scheme illustrates how systemic biologic agents, inhaled adrenergic drugs, and broader therapeutic groups are organized to reflect their pharmacologic actions and roles in the management of obstructive airway diseases.
